item.title: Hollywood Needs Regular Jolts of Creativity. It Just Got One.
item.description: The runaway success of horror films made by internet-bred creators shouldn’t scare Hollywood. It should inspire us.

item.title: The Debate Over Remote Work Isn’t Over
item.description: Readers respond to an Opinion guest essay, “We Liked Remote Work Until We Looked at the Data.” Also: The blame game in America today.

item.title: Trump’s Iran Debacle Could Be a Gift for America
item.description: There is at least a chance that America will not return to war with Iran, but find a way to turn the page on 47 years of animosity and confrontation.

item.title: President Narcissus and the Fetid Reflecting Pool
item.description: Why the president’s latest renovation project is so irresistible and resonant.

item.title: Thomas L. Friedman on the Clash at the Core of the Iran Deal
item.description: How the world views of Jared Kushner and the founder of the Islamic Republic of Iran can help explain the issue at the heart of the negotiations.

item.title: ‘I Love Boosters’ Is the Perfect Film for Our Savage Era
item.description: “I Love Boosters” is a brilliant sendup of the absurd contradictions of our age.

item.title: Kevin Warsh Is Missing Alan Greenspan’s Point
item.description: Fed chair Kevin Warsh shouldn’t point to Alan Greenspan’s experience to bolster his argument that A.I. will allow the central bank to keep interest rates low.

item.title: U.S. Support Is Israel’s True Weakness
item.description: Israel’s dependence on the United States has allowed it to ignore the need for diplomacy.

item.title: If You Love America, Cringe for It
item.description: Let’s remember who we once were, because it’s what we may yet be again.

item.title: In Colombia, a Right-Wing Wildcard Rises
item.description: A right-wing victory in Latin America’s third-largest country could ripple across the region.

item.title: What’s the Matter With Congress?
item.description: A debate between old friends about whether it is fear or polarization that is making Congress weak.

item.title: The Pool That Reflects Trump’s Presidency
item.description: Readers respond to articles about the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool. Also: The politics of fine dining; learning something new.

item.title: How Tehran Won the World
item.description: Iran’s defiance in the face of Western aggression has become a rallying cry.

item.title: Social Security Is Running Out of Money, Fast
item.description: To save the program, we need to eliminate the payroll tax cap.

item.title: Covid Politics Don’t Have to Be Toxic
item.description: Nirav Shah is something that would seem anathema in contemporary politics: a public health technocrat who became a household name during Covid.

item.title: Is This the Year Florida Turns Blue?
item.description: David Jolly must persuade voters who have been increasingly hostile to Democrats that he’s a different kind of Democrat.

item.title: A Malicious Chapter in the History of American Justice
item.description: The Trump administration is the nation’s chief threat to the rule of law.

item.title: 10 Years After Brexit, the Dismal Verdict Is In
item.description: Brexit was supposed to let Britain return to a time when it still counted as a global power. A decade later, the costs are blindingly apparent.

item.title: The Radical Act of Enjoying This Life
item.description: On “The Ezra Klein Show,” the Times Opinion columnist Ezra Klein asks the novelist Gary Shteyngart about his philosophy of enjoyment and why it can feel like such a radical act to simply be present.

item.title: Why Democrats Need a Politics of Joy
item.description: The joylessness of metrics-obsessed, achievement-oriented elite culture is eroding the simple joys of living, the novelist Gary Shteyngart argues on “The Ezra Klein Show.” And, he says, this antipathy toward pleasure is a problem for Democrats.

item.title: Taking Trump to Task Over the Iran War
item.description: Readers react to President Trump’s threats against Iran, and other aspects of the war. Also: A memory of Dad; the New York-New Jersey tussle.

item.title: The ‘No Child Left Behind’ Nostalgia Is Delusional
item.description: Test scores are poor measures of an education.

item.title: The Secret Reason Bosses Want Everyone Back in the Office, Every Day of the Week
item.description: It’s all about them.

item.title: The Moment Alan Greenspan Should Be Remembered For
item.description: A crucial episode in the famous Fed chair’s legacy.

item.title: A.I. Doomaxxing Is Bad for Our Economy
item.description: The economic impact of A.I. is less concerning than consumers’ fear-based reactions to it.

item.title: Doctors Can Test Your Baby for Hundreds of Genetic Anomalies. Should They?
item.description: A National Institutes of Health study will evaluate whether to screen tens of thousands of healthy infants for genetic diseases.

item.title: Elon Musk Is Too Rich. We’ve Known That Since Plato.
item.description: The philosopher made a radical proposal about wealth.

item.title: Elon Musk Is Too Rich. We’ve Known That Since Plato.
item.description: The philosopher made a radical proposal about wealth.

item.title: Creature From the Green Lagoon
item.description: Trump’s slimy, stinky swamp within the swamp.

item.title: Creature From the Green Lagoon
item.description: Trump’s slimy, stinky swamp within the swamp.

item.title: ‘A Deeply Anxious Personality’: 3 Opinion Writers on JD Vance’s New Book
item.description: Three Opinion writers share their views on “Communion,” the vice president’s new book.

item.title: My Father’s Death Was the Start of My Life
item.description: It was the worst thing that ever happened to me. It also taught me everything I needed to live.

item.title: My Father’s Death Was the Start of My Life
item.description: It was the worst thing that ever happened to me. It also taught me everything I needed to live.

item.title: Wait, There Are Two Trump Administrations Right Now?
item.description: The center of American empire is not land but a collection of interlocked state agencies and their business constituencies.

item.title: Why We Delude Ourselves About the Birthrate
item.description: Low fertility rates are a lagging indicator, the final outcome of a string of social shifts.

item.title: ‘Crony Capitalism on Steroids’: 3 Writers on the World Cup’s First Week
item.description: The history of global soccer, in some sense, is a history of the world’s political problems.

item.title: Iran Just Taught the World a Dangerous Lesson
item.description: Tehran is the newly empowered gatekeeper of the world’s most important waterway for energy. Other countries have taken note.

item.title: ‘There Was Love, and Then There Was Suffering’: A Q&A With Christian Wiman
item.description: “The minute you are convinced that you have grasped something true about God, it dissolves in you.”

item.title: Menopause and the Failure of Women’s Health Care
item.description: Readers respond to “Menopause Should Not Be Such a Mystery,” an Opinion guest essay by Melinda French Gates.

item.title: What I Learned About Parenting as a Trans Dad
item.description: Becoming a father taught Zach Ellams how to overcome shame.

item.title: You Can’t Be a Superpower Without Allies
item.description: Even the most powerful state in the world is not all that powerful when it decides to go it alone.

item.title: Obama and Mamdani Show How It’s Done
item.description: Shadow commemorations in Chicago and New York.

item.title: Trump Was Right. The War Ended With a Surrender.
item.description: Each generation seems destined to learn anew the lesson that when hawks declare that a war will be painless, beware.

item.title: Trump Was Right. The War Ended With a Surrender.
item.description: Each generation seems destined to learn anew the lesson that when hawks declare that a war will be painless, beware.

item.title: What Makes JD Vance Tick?
item.description: Is deep-seated anxiety what’s driving JD Vance? The Opinion writer Michelle Cottle argues on “The Opinions” that the vice president’s new book, “Communion,” reveals that he remains trapped by childhood scars and a hypocritical obsession with the elite institutions he publicly denounces.

item.title: We Need to Keep Funding Weird Science
item.description: Science turned a serendipitous finding about lizard venom into one of the most important drugs of the century, but that type of research is getting harder to do.

item.title: We Need to Keep Funding Weird Science
item.description: Science turned a serendipitous finding about lizard venom into one of the most important drugs of the century, but that type of research is getting harder to do.

item.title: Will the Real JD Vance Please Stand Up?
item.description: Three Opinion writers share their views on “Communion,” the vice president’s new book.

item.title: Art Galleries Are Not OK
item.description: Many gallerists are questioning the fundamentals of their business. It’s as if the art world they know has fallen off its axis.

item.title: There Is No ‘Compromise’ on Abortion Rights
item.description: Readers respond to “The Missing Middle in the Abortion Debate,” an Opinion guest essay by Zaid Jilani.

item.title: Boycott Trump’s Ridiculous July 4 Party
item.description: The president is planning another self-aggrandizing spectacle.

item.title: Trump Put His Own Interests Above All in the Iran Deal
item.description: The Iran deal wasn’t about peace but answering Trump’s personal needs.

item.title: I Keep Telling People We’re Living in This Dystopian Novel
item.description: The 2010 novel “Super Sad True Love Story” foreshadowed our current world. So I asked its author, Gary Shteyngart, how he sees the world today and how we might find pleasure in it.

item.title: I Keep Telling People We’re Living in This Dystopian Novel
item.description: The 2010 novel “Super Sad True Love Story” foreshadowed our current world. So I asked its author, Gary Shteyngart, how he sees the world today and how we might find pleasure in it.

item.title: In Britain, the Far Right Is Moving to the Mainstream
item.description: Its opponents urgently need to demonstrate that they can do more than throw up their hands in horror.

item.title: The Verdict on Biden Is In
item.description: Preliminary judgments on Joe Biden’s one-term presidency, including Jill Biden’s, are all the rage.

item.title: We Ran the Numbers. Remote Work Is Bad for Us.
item.description: Despite its advantages, work-from-home arrangements have significantly deepened Americans’ isolation and distress.

item.title: The ‘Rivalry’ Between Vance and Rubio
item.description: There’s no “intense rivalry behind the scenes” between JD Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio, the vice president argues on “Interesting Times.” The Opinion columnist Ross Douthat asks the vice president what happens to his political future if the Iran deal goes poorly.

item.title: JD Vance: ‘I’m Not Fighting With the Pope’
item.description: Did JD Vance privately agree with the pope on the Iran war while publicly fighting with him? Ross Douthat asks the vice president on “Interesting Times.”

item.title: Why the Knicks Really Won
item.description: Teamwork isn’t just useful, the Knicks showed. It’s a higher calling.

item.title: JD Vance on the Morality of the Trump Administration
item.description: I asked the vice president what is Christian about this White House.

item.title: The Real Reason Trump Never Stops Talking About Voter Fraud
item.description: “Voter fraud” is not about fraud. It is about who votes and how.

item.title: Dear A.I. Companies, the Doom Trolling Needs to Stop
item.description: The major A.I. companies keep telling us how dangerous their new models can be, yet they must keep on building them.

item.title: Bob Dylan and Liza Minnelli Already Turned 80. They Have Thoughts for Trump.
item.description: “I feel the same as I did 50 years ago,” President Trump said. “It’s crazy.”

item.title: Trump Lost The War He Started In Iran
item.description: The United States is emerging weaker — militarily, diplomatically and economically — than at the start of the war.

item.title: The U.S. Is the Future of Soccer, for Better or Worse
item.description: Thanks to immigrants and the internet, Americans gained entry to a universe beyond our borders.

item.title: One City Might Have Just Cracked the Housing Crisis
item.description: A large real estate development in the middle of Vancouver, British Columbia, shows how cities can build more housing.

item.title: Detroit Is the Last City That Knows How to Dance
item.description: Dance floors are flooded with selfies and poseurs. But in one city, it’s still a place to feel free.

item.title: Does U.F.O. Disclosure Threaten Faith?
item.description: Steven Spielberg’s ‘Disclosure Day’ and the God-and-aliens debate.

item.title: Jeffrey Epstein Haunts the White House
item.description: A sleazy situation that Trump can’t wish away.
